name: Steve Mann WearCam / ShootingBack
short_description: The earliest sustained wearable always-on camera, deliberately inverting surveillance — the conceptual origin of the wearable-camera privacy debate.
long_description: Beginning in 1994, Steve Mann wore a wireless wearable camera continuously, transmitting first-person video to the internet in near real time. The practice, which he later named 'sousveillance' (French: observation from below), framed the camera not as a recording device but as a counter-surveillance instrument — citizens 'shooting back' at institutions that surveilled them. Decades before Google Glass or Ray-Ban Meta, WearCam posed every foundational question the field now debates: who consents when a wearable always-on camera enters shared space, where the footage goes, and whether symmetric access to recording changes power. The ShootingBack page at wearcam.org documents Mann's theoretical and activist framing of this work.
ai_note: No AI or ML in the system; WearCam is a camera-and-transmission rig with analogue/digital video and a radio link. Its significance is the sousveillance concept and the privacy questions it posed, not machine intelligence.

images.caption: Steve Mann wearing the wearable wireless webcam on December 13, 1994, with a visual filter applied to the transmitted image.
images.alt_text: A self-portrait of Steve Mann taken on December 13, 1994, showing him wearing an early wearable camera system. The image has a visible filter effect applied, indicating real-time video processing. Mann is seen from slightly above, wearing the camera rig on his head.

sources.title: ShootingBack — WearCam
sources.access_notes: Primary project page documenting the sousveillance / ShootingBack concept and practice.

sources.title: Smart clothing: The wearable computer and WearCam
sources.access_notes: Mann's key early paper describing the WearCam system and its wearable computing context. 249 citations (Google Scholar).

sources.title: 'WearCam' (The wearable camera): personal imaging systems for long-term use in wearable tetherless computer-mediated reality
sources.publisher: IEEE — International Symposium on Wearable Computers (ISWC)
sources.access_notes: Conference paper explicitly defining WearCam as a sustained personal imaging system. 145 citations (Google Scholar).

sources.title: Sousveillance: Inventing and Using Wearable Computing Devices for Data Collection in Surveillance Environments
sources.access_notes: The foundational paper coining and defining sousveillance; establishes the theoretical basis for ShootingBack.

sources.title: 'Reflectionism' and 'Diffusionism': New Tactics for Deconstructing the Video Surveillance Superhighway
sources.access_notes: Mann's essay in the art/technology journal Leonardo, framing the camera as a counter-surveillance tool. 129 citations (Google Scholar).

sources.title: Steve Mann (inventor) — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Documents the 1994–1996 continuous personal broadcasting experiments and Mann's institutional affiliations (MIT Media Lab, University of Toronto).

interactions.title: Walk into a store and flip the camera on the cameras
interactions.description: Mann would enter surveillance-heavy environments — department stores, government buildings — wearing the camera so that security footage of him was simultaneously captured of them, making the power of observation reciprocal.

interactions.title: Broadcast first-person video to the internet in real time
interactions.description: From 1994 onward, Mann transmitted his wearable camera's feed wirelessly to a server visible on the early web, making his daily life the subject of one of the first continuous personal broadcasts — before YouTube, podcasting, or social media livestreaming existed.

interactions.title: Apply a visual filter to your own view of the world
interactions.description: WearCam was paired with EyeTap optics that could mediate Mann's own vision — showing him a processed or filtered version of the scene in real time, not just recording for others but augmenting his own perception.
interactions.source_label: Mann (1997) — Smart clothing: The wearable computer and WearCam

interactions.title: Ask a security guard why you're being filmed if they can't film you
interactions.description: Mann used the visible presence of his wearable camera as a conversational tool: by making it clear he was recording, he could ask security personnel to justify their own cameras — making the ethical asymmetry visible and discussable.

interactions.title: Use always-on capture as a lifelogging memory prosthetic
interactions.description: Beyond political activism, continuous capture served as autobiographical memory: Mann could search his video record for details of past conversations or experiences, anticipating by decades the lifelogging products (Narrative Clip, Limitless Pendant) that would commercialize the idea.
